title: Local aspects of Venkatesh's thesis.
speaker: Yiannis Sakellaridis
abstract: The thesis of Akshay Venkatesh obtains a ``Beyond Endoscopy'' proof of stable functorial transfer from tori to ${\rm SL}(2)$, by means of the Kuznetsov formula. In this talk, I will show that there is a local statement that underlies this work; namely, there is a local transfer operator taking orbital measures for the Kuznetsov formula to test measures on the torus. The global comparison of trace formulas is then obtained as a Poisson summation formula for this transfer operator.

proof_stages.label: Local functorial character transfer for a quadratic torus
proof_stages.summary: The talk fixes the basic local setting of a quadratic torus mapping by L-groups into GL2/PGL2 and recalls the resulting transfer of torus characters to stable characters of SL2, including the explicit Gelfand--Graev--Piatetski-Shapiro convolution formula.

proof_stages.label: Adjoint transfer on test measures and global trace-formula comparison
proof_stages.summary: Langlands' idea is formulated as a transfer operator on stable orbital-integral test measures, adjoint to character transfer, and then globalized as a comparison between the stable trace formula for SL2 and the Poisson trace formula for the torus, restricted to the functorial image.

proof_stages.label: L-function filtering and the move to Kuznetsov
proof_stages.summary: The functorial, dihedral representations are singled out by a pole of the appropriate adjoint/symmetric-square L-function, leading to a modified trace formula; Sarnak's observation motivates replacing the stable trace formula by the Kuznetsov formula, where the unwanted trivial representation is absent and the adjoint denominator is already built in.

proof_stages.label: Kuznetsov test spaces and insertion of L-functions
proof_stages.summary: The speaker explains the Kuznetsov formula first classically and then adelically as a functional on Whittaker-orbital-integral test measures, where inserting an L-function corresponds locally to evaluating the functional on a non-compactly-supported enlarged test measure with controlled behavior at infinity.

proof_stages.label: Main local transfer theorem for the enlarged Kuznetsov space
proof_stages.summary: The main local theorem asserts an explicitly described enlarged Kuznetsov test-measure space and an explicit transfer operator to Weyl-invariant torus test measures, characterized by pullback of relative characters, a fundamental lemma for the basic function and Hecke algebra, and compatibility with the global residue/Poisson-summation mechanism.

proof_stages.label: Endoscopic comparison and Fourier-transform nature of beyond-endoscopic transfer
proof_stages.summary: The theorem is compared with the Labesse--Langlands endoscopic transfer for a torus in SL2: both have character identities, a fundamental lemma, and trace-formula compatibility, but ordinary endoscopy is geometric orbit matching whereas the beyond-endoscopic transfer is given by Fourier-transform-type operations between the Kuznetsov variable and the torus trace variable.

examples.name: Split-torus principal-series character
examples.note: The speaker notes that in the split torus case the displayed convolution formula degenerates to the familiar character formula for a principal series representation, making the stable-character formula concrete without being used in the construction.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker only notes that in the split-torus case the displayed character formula becomes the principal-series character, without deriving or checking it

examples.name: Classical Patterson--Kuznetsov formula for fixed weight cusp forms
examples.note: A simplified holomorphic cusp-form version of the Kuznetsov formula is used to show concretely how Fourier coefficients, Hecke eigenvalues, and the adjoint denominator appear when one inserts a symmetric-square/adjoint L-function.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker writes the fixed-weight cusp-form Fourier expansion, relates coefficients to Hecke eigenvalues and the adjoint L-value, and sums Patterson--Kuznetsov formulas to insert the symmetric-square L-function
